blocks.text: A program that always does the same thing isn't very interesting. Conditionals let your code take different paths based on what's happening right now. This is how games respond to what you do, how apps show different things to different users, how anything interactive works.

blocks.desc: If this condition is true, run the code inside the curly braces
If the condition was false, run this instead
Check a second condition — only runs if the first one was false

lines: console.log('Good job! You got a B.');
// score is 75, so the second condition is true
// JavaScript checks top to bottom and stops at the first true one

blocks.label: Top to bottom — first match wins
blocks.text: JavaScript checks each condition in order. The moment it finds a true one, it runs that block and skips the rest. If you put a less specific condition first, a more specific one below it might never run.

exercises.title: Trace the path
tasks: Look at the starter code. There are 4 different values for score.
For each one, predict which message will print before you run it.
Run the code and check. Where were you wrong?
Change the score to exactly 70. Which branch runs? Why?
exercises.hint: Go through the conditions top to bottom. The first one that's true wins.

exercises.title: Weather advisor
tasks: Declare a variable called temperature and set it to 22.
Write an if / else if / else that logs:
- 'Bundle up!' if below 5
- 'Wear a jacket' if below 15
- 'Nice weather!' if below 25
- 'It's hot!' otherwise
Test with temperatures: 2, 10, 22, 35
exercises.hint: Order matters! Put the most specific (lowest) condition first.

checks.label: logged the correct message for 22 degrees

exercises.title: Build a quiz
tasks: Use prompt() to ask: 'What is 8 times 7?'
Convert the answer to a Number.
Use an if/else to check if the answer is 56.
If correct: log 'Correct! 8 x 7 = 56'
If wrong: log 'Not quite. The answer was 56. You said: ' + their answer
Test it with a right answer and a wrong answer.
exercises.hint: Number(prompt(...)) gets the input and converts it. Then compare with ===.
exercises.starterCode:
let answer = Number(prompt('What is 8 times 7?'));

// Check the answer

exercises.title: Multiple conditions with &&
tasks: Declare: playerLevel = 5, hasKey = true, bossDefeated = false
Write an if that logs 'You can enter the castle!' only when: level is 5 or higher AND hasKey is true.
Write a second if that logs 'Game over. Please restart.' when bossDefeated is false AND playerLevel is below 3.
Change the values and confirm the messages only appear under the right conditions.
Challenge: add an else to each if with an appropriate message.
exercises.hint: Combine conditions with &&: if (playerLevel >= 5 && hasKey === true)

challenges.title: The else block never runs
challenges.description: No matter what score is set to, only the first message prints. Why?
challenges.hint: Think about the first condition. Is score >= 0 true for any score?
challenges.explanation: score >= 0 is true for every possible score (including 40), so the very first branch always wins. The else if and else never get a chance to run. Fix: reorder so the most specific conditions come first — check >= 50 before >= 0.

challenges.title: The discount code only works sometimes
challenges.description: The store should give a discount when the customer has a coupon OR has spent more than $100. But it's only giving the discount when BOTH are true.
challenges.hint: Should both conditions need to be true, or just one?

checks.name: getsDiscount
checks.label: getsDiscount is true when hasCoupon is true

challenges.explanation: && requires both conditions. The customer has a coupon but only spent $50. Since amountSpent > 100 is false, the whole && expression is false. Change && to || so having either condition is enough.

challenges.title: The game never ends
challenges.description: The game should end when lives reach 0 OR score hits 100. Find the three bugs.
challenges.hint: Count the equals signs in the condition. What do single, double, and triple equals each do?
challenges.explanation: There are three = signs where there should be === (comparison, not assignment). lives = 0 SETS lives to 0 inside the condition — it doesn't check if lives IS 0. Fix all three: lives === 0 || score === 100.
